title: Williams v. Mississippi
summary: The Supreme Court upheld Mississippi's poll tax, literacy test, and related voter qualification scheme because the law did not explicitly name race on its face, even though it was designed and administered to disfranchise Black voters.
outcome_summary: The ruling legitimized facially race-neutral voter suppression tools that were central to Jim Crow disfranchisement across the South.
impact_notes: A foundational anti-voting-rights precedent because it gave constitutional cover to state schemes designed to strip Black citizens of the franchise.

scores.notes: One of the key Supreme Court decisions entrenching Jim Crow disfranchisement.

sources.notes: Decision text describing the Court's approval of Mississippi's voting qualification regime.

title: Giles v. Harris
summary: The Supreme Court refused to grant meaningful relief to Black plaintiffs challenging Alabama's disfranchising constitution, effectively declining to use federal judicial power to restore their voting rights.
outcome_summary: The ruling signaled that federal courts would not reliably intervene against systematic Black disfranchisement in the Jim Crow South.
impact_notes: Deepened the practical collapse of Reconstruction voting protections by leaving a major disfranchising regime in place.

scores.notes: A major judicial retreat from federal protection of Black suffrage.

sources.notes: Decision text showing the Court's refusal to meaningfully remedy Alabama disfranchisement.

title: Nixon v. Herndon
summary: The Supreme Court struck down a Texas statute that explicitly barred Black citizens from voting in Democratic primary elections.
outcome_summary: The ruling was an important but incomplete victory against white primaries, because states and party actors quickly adapted with new exclusion mechanisms.
impact_notes: A meaningful legal win against explicit racial disfranchisement, but one that did not end the white primary system on its own.

scores.notes: An important anti-Jim Crow voting-rights decision with limited durability.

sources.notes: Decision text holding Texas's explicit white-primary statute unconstitutional.

title: Grovey v. Townsend
summary: The Supreme Court upheld the exclusion of Black voters from a Texas Democratic primary by treating the party's action as private rather than state action.
outcome_summary: The decision preserved a central disfranchisement mechanism in the South until the Court reversed course in Smith v. Allwright in 1944.
impact_notes: A harmful Jim Crow precedent because it allowed white primaries to persist through formal distinctions between party and state action.

scores.notes: A major setback in the fight against white primaries, later overturned.

sources.notes: Decision text upholding exclusion from a Texas Democratic primary.

title: Social Security Act of 1935
summary: Created federal old-age insurance, unemployment insurance, and public assistance programs, but initially excluded many occupations in which Black workers were concentrated, including large portions of agricultural and domestic labor.
outcome_summary: The Act became a pillar of the modern welfare state, but its original exclusions limited Black workers' access to social insurance and reinforced racial inequality in economic security.
impact_notes: Historically transformative overall, but structured in ways that disproportionately excluded Black workers at the time of enactment.

scores.notes: A foundational federal welfare law whose racially unequal occupational exclusions had major downstream effects.

sources.source_title: Anniversary of the Social Security Act of 1935
sources.notes: Official overview confirming the Act's signature date and core program structure.
SSA history page explaining the original design of old-age insurance, unemployment insurance, and related provisions.
SSA history page describing the law's original coverage structure and early occupational limits.

title: United States Housing Act of 1937
summary: Created the United States Housing Authority and provided federal support for low-income housing and slum clearance, but administration through local authorities often reproduced segregation and unequal access.
outcome_summary: The law expanded the federal role in housing, but public-housing development and slum-clearance policy were frequently implemented within segregated local systems that limited equitable benefit for Black communities.
impact_notes: A major housing intervention that could provide relief, yet often reinforced segregation and unequal administration at the local level.

scores.notes: Important federal housing legislation whose practical effects for Black communities were constrained by segregated administration.

sources.notes: Statute compilation for the Wagner-Steagall Act creating the United States Housing Authority.
Historical congressional compilation identifying the Act and the federal housing authority it created.

title: Lane v. Wilson
summary: The Supreme Court struck down Oklahoma's replacement voter registration scheme because it preserved advantages for voters previously protected by a grandfather clause while imposing restrictive, short registration windows on Black citizens excluded under Jim Crow.
outcome_summary: The ruling invalidated one adaptation of disfranchisement after Guinn v. United States, though Southern states continued searching for other suppression techniques.
impact_notes: An important voting-rights decision because it recognized that replacement schemes could preserve racial disfranchisement even after an earlier grandfather clause was struck down.

scores.notes: A useful anti-disfranchisement ruling, though states continued to evolve suppression tools.

sources.notes: Decision text describing Oklahoma's replacement registration scheme after Guinn.
